claims.claim_text: A global e-commerce company had a specific 45-minute Cyber Monday checkout outage in 2020 caused by Kubernetes endpoint limits.
claims.anchor_text: In 2020, a global e-commerce giant faced a catastrophic 45-minute checkout outage during Cyber Monday.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At large scale, legacy Service-backend discovery can become a traffic-routing bottleneck during peak events.
claims.rationale: This is a specific real-world outage anecdote with precise timing and event framing but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The legacy Kubernetes Endpoints API truncates backend lists at 1,000 entries.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly document truncation of legacy Endpoints objects beyond 1,000 backing endpoints.

claims.claim_text: Because of that truncation, thousands of transactions were sent to an overloaded subset while newer replicas received no traffic.
claims.anchor_text: Thousands of transactions were forced through a stagnant subset of increasingly overloaded pods. Meanwhile, hundreds of newly scaled replicas sat completely idle, receiving zero traffic.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Any load-balancing path that still depends on the legacy `Endpoints` API can end up sending traffic to only a subset of the available backends once a Service exceeds the old object's capacity.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is real, but these incident-specific outcomes are presented as a concrete event without a source.

claims.claim_text: The company lost an estimated $3.2 million before an engineer found the over-capacity annotation and migrated to EndpointSlice.
claims.anchor_text: The company lost an estimated $3.2 million in revenue before a senior engineer identified the obscure `endpoints.kubernetes.io/over-capacity: truncated` annotation and migrated the service to the newer API.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In this kind of over-capacity situation, operators may discover the `endpoints.kubernetes.io/over-capacity: truncated` annotation and need to migrate clients and tooling to `EndpointSlice`.
claims.rationale: The revenue figure and incident resolution sequence are unsourced real-event specifics and must be removed.

claims.claim_text: As of Kubernetes v1.33+, reading or writing Endpoints objects emits deprecation warnings.
claims.rationale: The v1.33 deprecation post explicitly states that the API server returns warnings for Endpoints reads and writes.

claims.claim_text: Legacy Endpoints objects truncate at 1,000 items and add the `endpoints.kubernetes.io/over-capacity: truncated` annotation.
claims.rationale: The Service docs cover both the 1,000-endpoint truncation behavior and the over-capacity annotation.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes creates EndpointSlices with no more than 100 endpoints by default and creates a new slice when another endpoint must be added.
claims.rationale: Both the EndpointSlice and Service docs document the default 100-endpoint slice behavior.

claims.claim_text: kube-proxy uses EndpointSlices as its source of truth for internal traffic routing.
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice docs say EndpointSlices act as kube-proxy's source of truth, and the virtual IP docs say kube-proxy watches EndpointSlice objects.

claims.claim_text: The legacy Endpoints API lacks dual-stack and trafficDistribution support, truncates large endpoint lists, and EndpointSlices are stable since v1.21.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2: The Shift to EndpointSlices
claims.rationale: These versioned capability and limitation claims are covered across the Service, EndpointSlice, and v1.33 deprecation docs.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlices group endpoints by IP family, protocol, port, and Service name, and are automatically created for selector-based Services.
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice docs explicitly describe automatic creation and grouping semantics.

claims.claim_text: The module claims EndpointSlices can list up to 100 ports and that `FQDN` addressType has undefined semantics while only IPv4/IPv6 are processed by kube-proxy.
claims.anchor_text: The controller's `--max-endpoints-per-slice` limit can be configured up to 1,000 endpoints per slice, though each EndpointSlice may also list up to 100 ports. EndpointSlice `addressType` supports `IPv4`, `IPv6`, and `FQDN` (though FQDN currently has no defined semantics and only IPv4/IPv6 are processed by `kube-proxy`).
claims.suggested_rewrite: The controller's `--max-endpoints-per-slice` setting is configurable, and EndpointSlices are primarily used to represent Service backends in a more scalable form than legacy `Endpoints`.
claims.rationale: This sentence mixes partly supported details with unstable or inconsistently documented specifics and should be simplified.

claims.claim_text: Endpoints can transiently appear in more than one EndpointSlice because updates and watches arrive at different times.
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice docs have a dedicated section documenting duplicate endpoints across slices during transitions.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says legacy Endpoints have unlimited max entries while EndpointSlices have 100 per slice.
claims.anchor_text: | Max entries | Unlimited (but problematic) | 100 per slice |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Max entries | Single object with scalability limits | Small slices created and updated independently |
claims.rationale: The legacy Endpoints row is inaccurate because the old API truncates over-capacity data instead of being meaningfully unlimited.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlice routing uses `ready`, `serving`, and `terminating`; terminating endpoints are usually ignored but may still receive traffic if all available endpoints are terminating.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.4 Endpoints with NotReady Pods & EndpointSlice Conditions
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice docs define these conditions and document the all-terminating fallback.

claims.claim_text: For pod-backed endpoints, nil `ready` or `serving` values are interpreted as true, while nil `terminating` is interpreted as false.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.4 Endpoints with NotReady Pods & EndpointSlice Conditions
claims.rationale: The shared pool does not cover nil-default semantics, but the official Kubernetes API reference does.

claims.claim_text: Services without selectors do not get EndpointSlices created automatically and must use manual EndpointSlices or legacy Endpoints.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.1 When to Use Manual Endpoints
claims.rationale: The Service docs describe selectorless Services with manual EndpointSlices, and the EndpointSlice docs describe mirroring legacy Endpoints.

claims.claim_text: Manually managed EndpointSlices should use the `endpointslice.kubernetes.io/managed-by` label, and each EndpointSlice name must be unique within a namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.1 When to Use Manual Endpoints
claims.rationale: Both docs explicitly describe the `managed-by` label and the unique-name requirement for EndpointSlices.

claims.claim_text: A headless Service has no ClusterIP, and DNS returns the selected Pod IPs directly instead of a single virtual IP.
claims.span_hint: section: 5.1 What Is a Headless Service?
claims.rationale: The Service and DNS docs both document headless Service behavior and returned DNS records.

claims.claim_text: Selector-based headless Services create EndpointSlices automatically, while selectorless headless Services do not.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly distinguish headless Services with selectors from those without selectors.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlices support topology-aware routing so traffic can stay in the same zone to reduce latency and cross-zone cost, and `service.kubernetes.io/topology-mode: Auto` enables that behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6: Service Topology and Topology Hints
claims.rationale: The shared pool does not include Topology Aware Routing, but the official Kubernetes concept page directly covers same-zone routing and the `topology-mode` annotation.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says a manual Endpoint or EndpointSlice name must exactly match the Service name.
claims.anchor_text: | **Wrong endpoint name** | Endpoints drop entirely and are not associated with the target service. | The manual Endpoint or EndpointSlice name must exactly match the Service name. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Wrong endpoint linkage** | Manually created backends are not associated with the target Service. | For legacy Endpoints, the resource name must match the Service; for EndpointSlices, link them with the `kubernetes.io/service-name` label. |
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice part of the row is incorrect because EndpointSlices are linked by label, not by exact object name.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says `targetRef` should always be included when building manual endpoints for in-cluster resources.
claims.anchor_text: | **Missing `targetRef`** | You cannot dynamically trace an endpoint back to the pod that generated it. | Always include `targetRef` mapping when building manual endpoints pointing to inner-cluster resources. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Missing `targetRef`** | Tracing an endpoint back to its originating object becomes harder. | Add `targetRef` when you want easier debugging for in-cluster backends. |
claims.rationale: This is stronger than the API requires; `targetRef` is useful for traceability but not mandatory in all manual endpoint definitions.

claims.claim_text: Legacy Endpoints do not support modern dual-stack use cases and EndpointSlices should be used for IPv6 or dual-stack networking.
claims.rationale: The Service docs and the v1.33 deprecation post both state that Endpoints do not support dual-stack service features.

claims.claim_text: Only the affected EndpointSlice is updated during pod churn, reducing API server and kube-proxy processing time by roughly 30x.
claims.anchor_text: When a pod changes, only the affected slice (~100 entries) is updated and transmitted, reducing API server load and kube-proxy processing time by roughly 30x.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a pod changes, only the affected slice needs to be updated and transmitted, which substantially reduces API server and kube-proxy churn compared with rewriting one giant Endpoints object.
claims.rationale: The efficiency claim is sound, but the exact 30x figure is an illustrative back-of-the-envelope benchmark rather than a sourced measurement.

claims.claim_text: StatefulSets use a headless Service to give each Pod a stable DNS name like `<pod-name>.<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`.
claims.rationale: The shared pool does not cover StatefulSet pod identity, but the official StatefulSet docs do.

claims.claim_text: The quiz answer says nil `ready` or `serving` defaults to true and nil `terminating` defaults to false for pod-backed endpoints.
claims.rationale: This exact nil-default behavior is documented in the official Kubernetes API reference rather than the shared section pool.

claims.claim_text: If a Service exceeds 1,000 active pods, the legacy Endpoints API truncates at 1,000 and traffic paths that rely on Endpoints can strand the rest.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly describe truncation and the fact that legacy Endpoints-based load balancing only reaches up to 1,000 backends.

claims.claim_text: kube-proxy may still route to endpoints marked `serving: true` and `terminating: true` when all available endpoints are terminating.
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice docs directly describe this intended fallback behavior during rolling updates.

further_reading.title: EndpointSlices
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for slice sizing, conditions, duplication behavior, and controller-managed EndpointSlices.

further_reading.title: Service
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers selectorless Services, headless Services, deprecated Endpoints behavior, and over-capacity truncation.

further_reading.title: DNS for Services and Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how normal and headless Services resolve in cluster DNS, including multiple A/AAAA responses.

notes: Shared pool coverage is strong for Service and EndpointSlice mechanics. The unsourced outage story and two Common Mistakes rows need rewrite rather than citation. Nil-default EndpointSlice condition semantics required an official Kubernetes API reference URL outside the shared pool.
